content: The Operations Department at Police Headquarters plans and monitors peace and security matters across Nepal, receives information about the actual situation, reports to the Inspector General of Police about incidents, and makes reports as per suggestions and directives from the Inspector General of Police in accordance with the Chain of Command  this is the provision for obtaining information for any demonstration, sit-in, protest, or movement.

On Bhadra 23, I was working in the Human Resource Development Department at Nepal Police Headquarters. Since the movement was pre-announced, the District Security Committee had already made a threat assessment and approved a security plan beforehand, and thousands of police personnel were deployed accordingly. The Kathmandu Valley Police Office leadership had been vacant since Bhadra 18, and after some colleague DIG officers evaded responsibility  some unwillingly and some under the pretext of being unwell  I internalized the principle that in sensitive circumstances, a national servant must fulfill their role without reservation, and at approximately 09:00 AM on Bhadra 24, 2082, I arrived to take responsibility as Office Chief of the Valley Police Office as per verbal order (a letter was given to me on Bhadra 28).

Within moments of assuming responsibility, after being summoned by the ministry, I gave brief directives to senior officers and reached the Ministry of Home Affairs. Thereafter, continuously participating in various high-level security meetings including the Prime Minister's residence and Army Headquarters, I remained actively engaged in reporting, coordination, and directive roles. Decisions had to be made under time pressure and extreme pressure as violence, arson, vandalism, and attacks spread simultaneously across the Valley.

Since the Bhadra 23/24 Gen Z movement was a pre-announced program, monitoring the information of social media platforms including Reddit, Discord, and X used by the Gen Z members, a security plan was approved through the District Security Committee meeting and 4,396 Nepal Police personnel had been deployed.

Since my responsibility was at the Human Resource Development Department at Police Headquarters, it was not my work nature to analyze events  but by analyzing national/international events, as soon as I learned the news of Nepal's decision to close social media during the Gen Z movement, I called then Information and Communication Minister Prithivi Subba Gurung and suggested that since closing social media had affected online communication, business, and studies  leaving citizens frustrated  it would be better to apologize to the public and open it. After the Bhadra 23 incident, Minister Prithivi Subba Gurung also asked me at approximately 15:00 about what to do regarding the human casualties  I had expressed my views suggesting that resignation and similar actions would be appropriate reasons for the irreversible human casualties and to prevent further possible casualties.

After the tragic human casualties that occurred on Bhadra 23, the risk of the country heading toward another major conflict was clearly visible. Therefore, on Bhadra 24, the supreme security priority was set as protection of human lives. Against the backdrop of widespread condemnation of the Bhadra 23 events, with people calling it murder, massacre, and crimes against humanity (and the Home Minister having already resigned), in a situation where security agencies had to prioritize between human security and physical security for maintaining public peace and security, even though damage to structures like the Parliament building was unfortunate, since using force could potentially take the lives of thousands of innocent citizens, exercising restraint was unavoidable. In my view, the principle of 'Life over Property' was applied in this context.

The claims on social media and complaints received at this Commission that I made various attempts in my capacity as Valley Chief to bring Rabi Lamichhane, Chairperson of the Rastriya Swatantra Party who was in custody at Nakkhu Prison Lalitpur, outside are false, fictional, baseless, and come from malicious intent. Evidence is increasingly showing that one of the competitors engaged in unhealthy competition to become Inspector General of Police at that time deliberately planted that complaint. Since no law grants the Valley Police Chief the authority to release prisoners and detainees, no attempt was made outside the law. Court decisions and orders are also implemented only through the relevant agencies via the district court.

When some police offices caught fire during violent attacks and lives of detainees in custody were immediately endangered, directives to release detainees under the condition that they would appear when searched  while maintaining minimum standards of human rights and keeping records  were given only in limited and special circumstances. This was not any collective or indiscriminate order. Additionally, the allegation of only using tear gas is factually incorrect  rubber bullets, dummy rounds, and aerial fire were also used as necessary.

In that difficult situation, with limited manpower remaining at police offices and simultaneous attacks occurring, a large quantity of weapons, ammunition, and property was looted. However, through extensive searches and legal action, a large number of weapons and bullets have since been recovered, and perpetrators are being brought within the scope of legal action. My assessment is that because police personnel discharged their duty to protect citizens without caring for their own lives, much larger damage was prevented.

The fact that 3 police personnel died, and thousands of police and demonstrators were injured in these events is extremely painful. I express deep grief over all those events. The organization has been sensitive toward police personnel who worked bravely in the course of duty and toward martyr families, and appropriate honor and assistance has been provided.

Understanding the sentiments expressed in the movement, as both the then Valley Police Chief and now the head of the Nepal Police organization, I am confident that enforcing the law establishes the rule of law, the rule of law maintains good governance, and good governance establishes prosperity and civilization  and all my good efforts will be directed toward that establishment, and my efforts will not allow a conflict situation to develop again in the country. As a responsible professional police officer, many looted weapons and escaped prisoners have already been searched and brought within the legal framework. I am firm and committed to ending the atmosphere of fear and insecurity created among ordinary Nepali citizens, national servants, and justice workers by adopting public security strategies, motivating police personnel to remain loyal to the country and professionally fulfill duties according to public expectations, and maintaining peace, security, and law and order.

For the brave and honest performance of duty during the demonstrations on Bhadra 23 and 24, we immediately provided NPR 1 million each to the families of the 3 immortal police martyr families, gave one-rank promotion to 2 police personnel, and a large number have been rewarded with cash rewards recorded in official records. I consider it necessary to be vigilant about the negative impact on the force when nothing happens to irresponsible officers who fled and abandoned duty in difficult violent protest circumstances, while national servants who worked without caring for their lives for the country and its interests are questioned.

Due to the Bhadra 23 and 24 events, 1,618 police personnel working in various offices in the Kathmandu Valley were injured and 3 police personnel died. Of the injured, 1,617 have been discharged after treatment, while 1 police sub-inspector (Nagendra Prasad Patel) is still receiving treatment at the National Trauma Center.

Due to security personnel's gunfire or other reasons, 1,084 persons were injured in the Kathmandu Valley (1,323 injured across the country), and have been discharged after treatment  but 7 persons including Prakash Bahadur Bohora, Abishek Shrestha, and Mukesh Awasthi are receiving treatment at the National Trauma Center; Kamal Ghimire and Daulat Thapa at 612 Sinamangal; and Laxman Nepali and Santosh Bohora at T.U. Teaching Hospital Maharajgunj. 37 demonstrators died and 883 demonstrators were injured. Additionally, 7 persons died from burns at Chuchepati Bhatbhateni, 1 at Koteshwor Bhatbhateni, 1 at Ganga Dahal's house in Lalitpur, 1 at Udyan warehouse at Chandragiri 5 Haibision, and 1 at Hyatt Hotel  totaling 11 deaths by burning. Similarly, 25 persons died outside the Valley  (Bhadra 23: 2 by gunshot in Sunsari; Bhadra 24: 3 in Jhapa by gunshot totaling 5; Bhadra 24: 1 in Morang, 4 at Sunsari Bhatbhateni, 3 in Kaski, 1 in Parsa, and 1 in Chitwan totaling 10 deaths by burning; and 2 in Dhading, 3 in Ramechhap, and 5 in Banke totaling 10 prisoner and juvenile detainees). There are 12 bodies found in burned condition that have not yet been identified.

For internal investigation of events related to the Gen Z movement on Bhadra 23 and 24, the following committees have been formed from the Valley Police Office: a Crime Investigation, Analysis, and Scene Panchanama Committee under the chairmanship of Police SP Prabin Ghital; a Photo and Video Footage Collection and Analysis Committee under the chairmanship of Police DSP Anshu Kumar Singh; a Property Assessment and Inspection Committee under the chairmanship of Police SP Prabin Dhital; and a committee for searching and recovering looted weapons, ammunition, and other materials under the chairmanship of Police DSP Shaligram Sharma. Regarding the events of Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, a committee has been formed from Nepal Police Headquarters on Kartik 11, 2082 under the convenorship of a DIG.

Overall, this movement is a matter of serious self-reflection for the state, society, and security mechanism alike. The decisions made in the then circumstances were not easy, but my intent was centered on minimizing human casualties, maintaining public peace, and not letting the country go toward conflict again. In my capacity as head of the Nepal Police organization, I am fully committed to ending the fear and insecurity created among citizens by taking legal action on remaining weapons, escaped prisoners, and perpetrators.
32. Statement of Additional Inspector General of Police Siddhi Bikram Shah (Then Operations Department Chief):

I wish to clarify my role and understanding regarding the management of the demonstration called by the Generation-Z group in the capital on Bhadra 23 and 24, 2082, and the situation that developed during it. After seeing information about the demonstration spreading intensively through social media platforms like TikTok, Facebook Reels, and Discord a few days before it, I had informed the then Inspector General of Police in a timely manner and discussed necessary monitoring and preparedness. Although the received information increasingly indicated that the demonstration could be large, my understanding remained that the initial security preparedness could not be strengthened to the expected level because the government did not take the possible risk seriously enough. It had been estimated that demonstrations could occur in the prohibited zone and there could be clashes with police, but no assessment was made that the pressure created by advancing through the prohibited zone could reach a level that could not be stopped.

Initial security arrangements were primarily advanced according to the plan of the District Police Office Kathmandu and the Valley Police Office. I myself went to the field early in the morning and observed the security situation from Maitighar Mandala to New Baneswhar area and made arrangements to send additional police to locations with fewer personnel. Although I remained in continuous contact with Valley security chiefs, district police chiefs, and other senior officers, since many decisions went directly from the Inspector General of Police to field commanders, my role was mostly limited to coordination.

On Bhadra 23, after the demonstration started, the situation became progressively tense. I received information through media and communication sets that the crowd was creating excessive pressure toward the prohibited zone, risk had increased in the Parliament building area, and arson and vandalism had begun toward the south gate. After arson at Parliament's Gate No. 1, stone-pelting at police, and attempts to enter the premises by breaking through the compound wall occurred, a report was received that shots had been fired. The estimate was that there was a mixed crowd of aggressive demonstrators in front carrying stones, sticks, khukuris, rods, and other materials, and unarmed general public behind  information came that their number was approximately 25-30 thousand. Since the legal provision for the decision to fire is made by local administration and the relevant command post, I had no direct role in it. On that day, reports of force use came from Kaski, Jhapa, Sunsari, Lalitpur, Parsa, and Rupandehi in addition to Kathmandu. According to initial details, information came of 17 deaths, 327 injured, 211 police with minor injuries, 33 Armed Police Force with injuries, and 1 other  totaling 572 injured.

Since signs indicated the situation would not calm down after the incident and the movement could spread across the country the next day, I took initiatives to summon additional personnel from various districts to the Kathmandu Valley that very night. As the situation became increasingly complex on Bhadra 24, I coordinated with the Nepal Army Headquarters throughout the night about sector allocation and joint deployment of army teams. However, from early morning, as crowds began forcibly entering various police offices, party offices, and government structures, security personnel were under pressure. After the order not to fire lethal bullets from the Valley Police Office went through communication sets and demonstrators learned that police had been directed to keep themselves safe, their activities became more violent in my assessment.

On Bhadra 24, after the Police Headquarters itself began becoming a target of vandalism, I remained at the frontline to keep the morale of police inside the office high, to encourage the small teams formed at each gate for resistance, and to give directives to use tear gas and aerial fire as necessary to protect the office. Even though some senior officers had slipped away in civilian clothes, I stayed at the frontline and consider myself successful in keeping the office safe. In this context, at the Bhadra 26 meeting, the Inspector General of Police praised my role.

On Bhadra 23 and 24, Nepal Police across the country had fired 2,776 rounds of rubber bullets from shotguns, 2,920 blank rounds, 4,023 rounds from SLR, INSAS, and .303, and 8,654 tear gas shells  such statistics were received. On Bhadra 24, a report came that 1,281 weapons, 98,432 bullets, 1,315 rubber bullets, 2,220 blank rounds, 2,842 tear gas shells, 1,687 magazines, and 5,834 communication equipment were looted or lost to arson across the country. On that same day, details were received of 48 civilians (including those injured the previous day) and 3 police personnel dying, and 883 civilians and 1,804 police personnel being injured.

After orders to prepare a list of detainees and release them, not to fire lethal bullets, and to direct police to remain safe went through Victor-1's communication set, since police did not resist, demonstrators' morale rose and they engaged in vandalism, arson, and looting.

Even though the Generation-Z group called through social media to withdraw the demonstration, since persons of other age groups were seen to be involved in intensifying and advancing the violence, infiltration can be estimated. Overall, I have reached the conclusion that the main causes of the Bhadra 23 events were: the Gen Z matter not being addressed timely, the demonstration being taken as routine, intelligence about infiltration not being taken seriously, and security preparation being inadequate.
33. Statement of Deputy Inspector General of Police Om Bahadur Rana (Acting Chief of Valley Police Office on Bhadra 23):

I was serving in the responsibility of Acting Office Chief of the Kathmandu Valley Police Office, Ranipokhari, from Bhadra 19 to Bhadra 23, 2082. On Bhadra 24, after handing over the Office Chief responsibility to then DIG Dan Bahadur Karki, I was serving in the role of 2-IC (Second-in-Command) at that same office.

Since the program had been approved by the District Administration Office, organizers had given written commitments, and available information did not indicate any hostile activity, the security plan for the Bhadra 23 program was focused on normal crowd management with confidence that it would initially be peaceful. However, anticipating possible challenges, preparations were made for additional police deployment including police from Kathmandu, Lalitpur, and Bhaktapur. On that day, initially 3,441 police were deployed across the Valley and an additional 505 police were dispatched as the situation became abnormal.

The actual situation of the program developed in a different direction than expected. Although the crowd gathered in the Maitighar area initially appeared peaceful, it progressively became leaderless, impulsive, and aggressive. The environment suddenly deteriorated due to the mixture of outsider groups, unknown provocateurs, and purposeless violent tendencies along with children and adolescents in school uniforms. The crowd violated the prohibited zone and advanced toward the Parliament building and attacked public structures and police deployment. I experienced the situation of that day as violent energy that was unpredicted and suddenly transformed.

As demonstrators using stones, bricks, sticks, shovels, and hammers continued pushing toward the Parliament building and continuously striking security personnel, a real situation was created where security personnel's lives were endangered and security control could be lost. During this time, I received information through communication sets about the curfew order issued by Kathmandu administration. After the curfew order, I directed subordinate commanders to follow orders flawlessly, not to show excessive agitation, to minimize possible casualties, and to control only persons involved in anarchic activities. However, by that time the crowd had become completely uncontrolled and had spread in thousands.

According to prevailing law regarding gunfire, the order for lethal force use (firearm use) is made by the Chief District Officer. The decision to fire did not come from Nepal Police Headquarters, nor was it my personal order. I have mentioned in my statement that commanders in the field assessed the then circumstances and implemented necessary decisions. According to the report, on that day within the Valley 52 rounds of metal bullets, 417 rounds of rubber bullets, 117 blank rounds, and 1,017 rounds of tear gas shells were fired. Non-lethal options such as microphone announcements, water cannons, tear gas, and rubber bullets were actively used. But due to factors such as the severity of the crowd, lack of leadership, targeted attacks on the Parliament building from all sides, and the necessity of preserving security personnel's lives, a compulsory situation was created, and lethal force was used only after those options proved ineffective.

I have expressed deep grief over the deaths and injuries that occurred during the demonstrations. My clear view is that no security personnel desires human casualties. But the situation that determined the then decisions included clear risk of arson at the Parliament building, attempts to surround and disarm security personnel, and circumstances suggesting an objective to capture the state apparatus.

Widespread panic, fear, or morale decline was not visible among security personnel under my command. On the contrary, my conclusion is that they demonstrated the ability to maintain restraint and continue action even in difficult circumstances.

Regarding looting, arson, loss of weapons and ammunition, and destruction of vehicles and communication equipment at police offices across the Valley, I have mentioned two main causes. First, the available number of police was insufficient to face the enormous crowd size, use of improvised weapons, and anarchic violence. Second, since using excessive force could have increased the number of deaths and injuries on both sides, security personnel chose restraint while making sensitive decisions between self-defense and collective damage.

Due to the arson and vandalism of Bhadra 23 and 24, 219 police units within the Valley  including the Valley Police Office at Ranipokhari  were damaged, with estimated damage of approximately NPR 1,595,630,337. Details have been received of various types of rifles, SLRs, shotguns, multi-gas guns, gas guns, hornets, .22 magnums, INSAS rifles, revolvers, pistols, SMGs, and Non-Lethal Multi Weapons being lost, looted, or destroyed by fire. Similarly, statistics have been received of thousands of live and blank rounds being lost or destroyed, heavy and light vehicles and motorcycles being burned, and repeaters, base sets, mobile sets, handsets, and other communication equipment being destroyed or looted. 1,616 police personnel were injured due to stone-pelting and use of improvised weapons.
To develop Nepal Police as a strong, capable, and professional organization, I have presented the following recommendations:

content.content: Additional management of necessary resources and means,
Availability of modern technology for crowd control,
Arrangement of timely training for producing skilled manpower,
Special reform in recruitment, selection, and career development,
Transparency in transfer, promotion, service, and facilities,
Amending laws and regulations in line with the times to make the institution fully empowered,

Establish a resource-equipped special crowd control battalion with manpower of up to 2,500 in the Valley and 1,000 in each Provincial Police Office, which would not be deployed elsewhere except in emergency situations.

content: 34. Statement of Then Office Chief of District Police Office Kathmandu, Deputy Inspector General of Police Bishwa Adhikari:

At that time I was serving as the Chief of District Police Office Kathmandu (Senior Superintendent of Police) and am currently serving as Deputy Inspector General of Police in the Administration Department at Police Headquarters. My statement before the Commission is a detailed account of the extremely sensitive security situation that arose around the Parliament building and New Baneswhar area during the Gen Z generation's demonstration on Bhadra 23 and 24.

Although it was estimated that protest activities could occur in the situation where the then Government of Nepal had closed social media, no clear advance warning of any unexpected activity or serious security challenge had been received. Based on the conclusions of the District Security Committee and Central Security Committee meetings on Bhadra 22, a written self-declaration had been made that 3,000-5,000 persons would participate, the demonstration would be peaceful, a rally would proceed from Maitighar to the edge of the prohibited zone in Baneswhar and a corner meeting would be held back at Maitighar, there would be no entry into the prohibited zone, and no violent activities would be engaged in. Based on that estimate, a program of the Honorable President at the National Assembly Hall and a National Assembly session were also scheduled for Bhadra 23.

In coordination with the main parties submitting applications for the demonstration  Sudan Gurung, Anil Baniya, K.P. Khanal, Deepak Bohra, Victor Paudel of the 'Hami Nepal' group, and the current Honorable Minister Gupta and others  the 'Integrated Special Security Plan, 2082' was approved with confidence that the program would be peaceful. A Command Post was established at the District Administration Office in Babarmahel under the main command of the Chief District Officer for duty deployment. For security management, 1,965 from Nepal Police, 631 from Armed Police Force, and 230 from the National Investigation Department were deployed for intelligence collection. A permanent team of the Nepal Police Special Task Force was deployed for security of the extremely sensitive structure like the International Convention Center (Parliament building).

No advance warning regarding discussions or possible violent plans on digital platforms including Discord, Reddit, and X (Twitter) was available to the District Security Committee. At the Central Security Committee meeting held at the Ministry of Home Affairs on the evening of Bhadra 22, the Chief District Officer and I participated and estimated the demonstration would be peaceful. Since youth and students would be participating, directives were received to exercise restraint, use force as little as possible, and not to do 'heavy deployment' by keeping manpower close. A policy was established to use force progressively only if there was vandalism or arson in the prohibited zone.

The demonstration was scheduled to start at Maitighar according to the pre-approved program and hold a corner meeting at the same location. But as one section advanced toward the prohibited zone and became agitated and began stone-pelting, and after breaking through the barricade at approximately 11:45, water cannons were used to maintain peace. With attention to the risk of approximately 600 staff, Members of Parliament inside the Parliament building and the diesel storage tank, a Layered Security Plan was implemented  iron barricades, human barricades, reserve force, and special task force were deployed. However, I experienced the situation beginning to go out of control after continuous attacks from all four directions.

Demonstrators used stone-pelting, petrol bombs, Molotov cocktails, and improvised sharp weapons. Attempts to demolish walls, arson at gates, security personnel being injured, screams of 'they're trying to kill us' on communication sets, and arson of the Armed Police ambulance made the situation extremely difficult. A curfew order was issued in accordance with the Local Administration Act, 2028, with the consent of the Ministry of Home Affairs. My view is that gunfire was used only as a Last Resort under the Doctrine of Necessity, and waiting for a separate written order in the then circumstances was not practical.

'Total Withdrawal' was not possible, because doing so could have caused widespread casualties and irreparable damage to state structures. Therefore, field commanders were directed to use non-lethal measures  water cannons, baton charges, tear gas, etc. After exercising restraint for approximately two hours and violent attacks continued, additional force was used only in extremely difficult circumstances.

On the evening of Bhadra 23, at the Prime Minister's residence Baluwatar, a meeting was held with the Prime Minister, Home Minister, Defense Minister, Foreign Minister, Nepali Congress President, Home Secretary, chiefs of all four security agencies, the Chief District Officer, and me to review the events. Discussions were held about deploying the Nepal Army at Vital Installations and deploying other security personnel in the field with the assessment that the situation could become even more complex on the 24th.

In social media analysis, the fact that attack location mapping was being done through platforms like netakhor.vercel.app and preparations were being made to make the crowd violent by spreading 'fake news' came to light later. Information was received on the 24th about plans to defy curfew, attack senior leaders' residences, and display bodies.

From the morning of Bhadra 24, simultaneous planned attacks began at Kalanki, Sankhu, Gongabu, Balaju, Balkhu, Koteshwor, Jadibuti, Boudha, Chabahil, Naikap, Kirtipur, Budhanilkantha, and Parliament building surroundings. Local governments, police offices, and government establishments began experiencing arson and vandalism. After the attack on Harisiddhi Police Office, attacks spread to other units. After small units became unsafe, manpower was concentrated at circles and divisions. Keeping in mind the risk of arson at offices and the possibility of detainees in custody suffering collective casualties, following directives received from Victor Control (Kathmandu Valley Police Office, Ranipokhari), after risk assessment, 206 detainees were released at various times.

There was sometimes a time gap in Tactical Movement between Armed Police and Nepal Police, and coordination challenges arose due to SOP and command clearance procedures. My view is that if the Nepal Army had taken over static duty, Nepal Police could have been fully focused on crowd control, but the practical complexity of deployment modality and risk of lethal weapon use may have limited the expected assistance.

At that time, reorganizing security personnel back at barracks, lifting body documentation panchanama, or securing the scene and collecting bullet casings was extremely challenging, as the rioting crowd was surrounding and attacking from all sides.
